perturbation.original_text: Owner will invoice such fees to Charterer on a monthly basis and Charterer will pay such invoiced amounts within 30 days of invoice date.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner will invoice such fees to Charterer on a monthly basis and Charterer will pay such invoiced amounts within 60 days of invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms from 30 to 60 days could introduce inconsistencies because longer payment terms might not align with standard or legally required payment timelines under Texas law, especially if specific industries have regulations mandating faster payment cycles.
perturbation.law_citation: Texas Business and Commerce Code Section 28.004
perturbation.law_explanation: The Texas Prompt Payment Act mandates specific payment deadlines for certain types of contracts. While this Transportation Services Agreement may or may not fall under the Act's purview, depending on the specifics of the fuel transportation and the nature of the parties involved, a 60-day payment term could potentially violate the Act if it is determined to be applicable, which often requires payment within 30 days. The modification creates an uncertainty of compliance.
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the payment term from 30 to 60 days, which, according to the law explanation, could violate the Texas Prompt Payment Act if that act applies to this agreement. The Perturbation Explanation also states that this change could introduce inconsistencies. Since this is categorized as 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction', and the change introduces a payment deadline that could violate state law, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Either party hereto shall have the right to terminate this Agreement in the event of a breach by the other party of its obligations hereunder, subject to 10 days prior written notice of such breach given by the non-breaching party to the breaching party and the opportunity for such breaching party to cure such breach during such 10 day period.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party hereto shall have the right to terminate this Agreement in the event of a breach by the other party of its obligations hereunder, subject to 5 days prior written notice of such breach given by the non-breaching party to the breaching party and the opportunity for such breaching party to cure such breach during such 5 day period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 10 to 5 days might be considered unreasonably short, potentially conflicting with general principles of contract law that require a fair opportunity to cure a breach. In some cases, courts may find a 5-day cure period to be insufficient, especially for complex breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Opportunity to Cure
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, general contract law requires that parties be afforded due process, which can include a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach before termination. Reducing the cure period to 5 days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the nature of the breach and could render the termination clause unenforceable. It has to deal with principles of fairness, reasonableness and good faith. A shorter cure period could violate these principles, especially if the breach involves complex issues or requires more time to remedy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: due process | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you due process What is due process? Due process (or due process of law) primarily refers to the concept found in the Fifth Amendment to the US Constitution, which says no one shall be "deprived of life, liberty or property without due process of law" by the federal government. The Fourteenth Amendment , ratified in 1868, uses the same eleven words, called the Due Process Clause, to extend this obligation the the states. These words have as their central promise an assurance that all levels of American government must operate within the law ("legality") and provide fair procedures. There are at least three other uses of the phrase "due process" in American constitutional law, and this article will also touch on each briefly. Incorporation The Fifth Amendment's reference to “due process” is only one of many promises of protection the Bill of Rights gives people against the federal government. Originally these promises had no application at all against the states; the Bill of Rights was interpreted to only apply against the federal government, given the debates surrounding its enactment and the language used elsewhere in the Constitution to limit State power. (see Barron v City of Baltimore (1833)). However, this changed after the enactment of the Fourteenth Amendment and a string of Supreme Court cases that began applying the same limitations on the states as the Bill of Rights. Initially, the Supreme Court only piecemeal added Bill of Rights protections against the States, such as in Chicago, Burlington & Quincy Railroad Company v. City of Chicago (1897) when the court incorporated the Fifth Amendment's Takings Clause into the Fourteenth Amendment. The Court saw these protections as a function of the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment only, not because the Fourteenth Amendment made the Bill of Rights apply against the states. Later, in the middle of the Twentieth Century, a series of Supreme Court decisions found that the Due Process Clause "incorporated" most of the important elements of the Bill of Rights and made them applicable to the states. If a Bill of Rights guarantee is " incorporated " in the "due process" requirement of the Fourteenth Amendment, state and federal obligations are exactly the same. For more information on the incorporation doctrine, please see this Wex Article on the Incorporation Doctrine . Substantive due process The words “due process” suggest a concern with procedure rather than substance, and that is how many—such as Justice Clarence Thomas, who wrote "the Fourteenth Amendment’s Due Process Clause is not a secret repository of substantive guarantees against unfairness" —understand the Due Process Clause. However, others believe that the Due Process Clause does include protections of substantive due process—such as Justice Stephen J. Field, who, in a dissenting opinion to the Slaughterhouse Cases wrote that "the Due Process Clause protected individuals from state legislation that infringed upon their ‘privileges and immunities’ under the federal Constitution” (see this Library of Congress Article: https://www.loc.gov/exhibits/magna-carta-muse-and-mentor/due-process-of-law.html ) Substantive due process has been interpreted to include things such as the right to work in an ordinary kind of job, marry, and to raise one's children as a parent. In Lochner v New York (1905), the Supreme Court found unconstitutional a New York law regulating the working hours of bakers, ruling that the public benefit of the law was not enough to justify the substantive due process right of the bakers to work under their own terms. Substantive due process is still invoked in cases today, but not without criticism (See this Stanford Law Review article to see substantive due process as applied to contemporary issues). The promise of legality and fair procedure Historically, the clause reflects the Magna Ca
perturbation.justification: The change from a 10-day to a 5-day cure period creates a legal contradiction because it may not provide a 'reasonable opportunity to cure a breach,' potentially violating principles of fairness and good faith as described in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, the provided excerpt from Wex defines 'due process' as ensuring government operates within the law and provides 'fair procedures'. Reducing the cure period could be seen as undermining fair procedure, depending on the context of the breach. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that a 5-day period might be deemed insufficient, especially for complex breaches, leading to enforceability issues.

perturbation.original_text: Owner covenants that it will maintain at all times during the Term of this Agreement insurance coverage for sudden and accidental pollution of $500,000,000.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner covenants that it will maintain at all times during the Term of this Agreement insurance coverage for sudden and accidental pollution of $10,000,000.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the pollution insurance coverage from $500,000,000 to $10,000,000 may not meet the financial responsibility requirements for oil transportation, especially in coastal areas. This modification could violate federal regulations related to oil spill liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Oil Pollution Act of 1990 (OPA 90) establishes liability and compensation requirements for oil spills. While it doesn't explicitly state a single minimum insurance amount for all cases, it requires that vessel owners and operators demonstrate sufficient financial responsibility to cover potential cleanup costs and damages. Depending on the size and type of vessels and the nature of the oil being transported, $10,000,000 may be insufficient to meet these requirements, especially considering the potential for large-scale spills and associated costs, thus contradicting the intent and potentially the specific requirements under OPA 90. The modification therefore introduces a compliance risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Oil Spill Prevention and Preparedness Regulations | US EPA Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. JavaScript appears to be disabled on this computer. Please click here to see any active alerts . Oil Spill Prevention and Preparedness Regulations Technical Alert: Failures of Overfill Prevention at Substantial Harm Oil Facilities EPA is issuing this Technical Alert to address the causes and contributing factors associated with significant oil discharge incidents in order to prevent their recurrence. Read the Alert Prevention and Preparedness One of EPA’s top priorities is to prevent, prepare for, and respond to oil spills that occur in and around inland waters of the United States. EPA has regulatory programs that help facilities prevent and prepare for discharges of oil. Wildfire Guide for Tanks This guide is a resource for underground storage tank and oil aboveground storage tank owners and operators in the event of a wildfire. View the guide 1 2 3 EPA's oil spill prevention program includes the Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) rule and the Facility Response Plan (FRP) rule. EPA is the lead federal response agency for oil spills occurring in inland waters. The U.S. Coast Guard is the lead response agency for spills in coastal waters and deepwater ports. The SPCC rule helps facilities prevent a discharge of oil into navigable waters or adjoining shorelines. Learn about SPCC The FRP rule requires certain facilities to prepare and submit a response plan for a worst case oil discharge or threat of a discharge. Learn about FRP EPA provides guidance for the regulated community and regional inspectors. Explore guidance Webinars and training for oil spill prevention and preparedness programs. Explore training Most Requested Documents A Facility Owner/Operator's Guide to Oil Pollution Prevention 40 CFR 112 Bulk Storage Container Inspection Fact Sheet SPCC Guidance for Regional Inspectors - Chapter 4: Secondary Containment and Impracticability Determinations Area Contingency Planning Handbook Tribal Oil Spill Prevention and Chemical Emergency Preparedness Webinars Related Information National Contingency Plan (NCP) NCP Subpart J Area Contingency Planning Waters of the United States Oil Discharge Reporting Requirements Contact Us to ask a question, provide feedback, or report a problem. Last updated on April 15, 2025
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the insurance coverage for pollution from $500,000,000 to $10,000,000. The Law Explanation states that the Oil Pollution Act of 1990 (OPA 90) requires vessel owners and operators to demonstrate sufficient financial responsibility to cover potential cleanup costs and damages, and that $10,000,000 may be insufficient depending on the potential for large-scale spills. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions EPA's regulatory programs to help facilities prevent and prepare for discharges of oil, including the Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) rule and the Facility Response Plan (FRP) rule. Given the potential costs associated with oil spill cleanup and damages, reducing the coverage introduces a risk of non-compliance with OPA 90, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable. Thus, a legal contradiction exists.
